nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef areas adjacent to sand or seagrass, estuaries and protected muddy bays; juveniles are commonly reported from mangrove environments. Generally encountered at depths of approximately 1–25 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on invertebrates including molluscs, crustaceans, sponges, and coral polyps in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts squid, krill, clams, mysis, and hard-shelled shrimp. Hard-shelled prey is commonly advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like teeth.

summary: The Reticulated Puffer is a large Indo-West Pacific pufferfish known for its dark brown body marked with a fine white net-like pattern. Generally considered semi-aggressive and not reef-safe, it is best kept singly in a large fish-only system with robust tankmates.
careNotes:
Arothron reticularis ranges across the Indo-West Pacific from India through Indonesia to southern Japan and northern Australia, and tends to inhabit shallow reef areas adjacent to sand or seagrass; juveniles are commonly reported from mangroves and estuaries. The species is distinguished by a dark brown body marked with a fine white reticulated (net-like) pattern, often with a dark spot bordered by concentric white lines around each pectoral fin base.

Like other Tetraodontidae, this species has fused beak-like teeth that grow continuously; hard-shelled foods are generally advised to help keep the beak worn down. Pectoral, dorsal, and anal fins are used for locomotion as the species lacks pelvic fins, but it remains highly maneuverable.

The Reticulated Puffer produces a heavy bioload and is intolerant of poor water quality, making robust filtration and a protein skimmer essential. Its diet in the wild includes sponges, clams, ornamental shrimp, snails, urchins, sea stars, and tubeworms, leaving it incompatible with reef systems.

As with all Arothron pufferfishes, the species is capable of inflating its body by ingesting water when threatened and should not be allowed to inhale air during transfers; a container rather than a net is generally advised. The flesh and organs contain tetrodotoxin and are highly poisonous to eat. It has been reported to be aggressive toward conspecifics and similar-shaped species, and is generally kept singly. Breeding has not been documented in home aquaria.
breedingNotes: Breeding has not been reported in home aquaria. The species is oviparous in the wild; no captive-breeding protocols are documented in the available aquarium literature.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary care sheet: adult size 16 in, minimum tank 150 gal, semi-aggressive, not reef-safe, T 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, carnivore. Care level listed as easy.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Tetraodontidae, Tetraodontiformes), max size (45 cm SL), depth range (1–25 m), distribution (Indo-West Pacific: India to Japan to N Australia), temperature (25.1–29.3°C, mean 28.6°C), oviparous, IUCN Least Concern, poisonous to eat.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm), temperature (25–28°C), minimum tank size (~2500 L), care level (only for advanced aquarists), corallivorous and invertebrate-eating diet. Tank-size figure is the high end of the spread; final 1000 L is biased larger per AquaMate convention rather than strict average.

additional.name: Quality Marine
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~18 in / 45.7 cm), minimum tank size (180 gal / ~680 L), temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, care level moderate, temperament aggressive, not reef-safe.

additional.name: Reef Guide (wild reference)
additional.notes: Wild reference: confirmed max size ~43 cm, Indo-West Pacific reef distribution, depth 3–18 m. Used for taxonomic and range cross-check only.

fish.breedingNotes: Not reported in home aquaria. Oviparous in the wild.
